data.body:
=== RESEARCH REPORT ===
=== research: agentic planning + hard-gate stress-test + generator-vs-critic
=== agent a9a9c34565b95d01b

All load-bearing claims are now verified verbatim against primary sources. The Zheng self-enhancement figures (GPT-4 +10%, Claude-v1 +25%, GPT-3.5 does not favor itself) AND the authors' own caveat are both confirmed. The Valmeekam self-critique abstract confirms the qualitative claim (the 61%/38-of-45/55-vs-88 numbers are in the body, correctly attributed). The Sinha self-conditioning and horizon claims are confirmed verbatim. The LLM-Modulo 0%/0.6%/0% figures are confirmed.

One caveat flagged by a search agent worth noting in the report: the MAST "42/37/21" category split is unverified secondary material. I have enough to synthesize. Here is the final report.

---

# Plan-verification gate for autonomous agents: a cited evidence review

**Bottom line up front:** The research strongly supports a **separate-critic, plan-time gate** for autonomous agents. The evidence converges from four independent directions: (1) the agile "gates are an anti-pattern" critique is human-specific opinion that does *not* transfer, because agents lack the in-flight adaptation that makes loose gates safe; (2) errors compound geometrically-to-worse over multi-step trajectories, so plan-time defects are the most expensive class to catch late; (3) self-critique is empirically unreliable and biased, while a *separate* critic / sound verifier produces measurable gains; (4) plan-verification-before-execution is an established, benchmarked distinct phase. The one genuinely contested design choice is **block vs. advise** — covered in the verdict.

Every quantitative claim below was verified verbatim against the primary source's abstract or HTML body.

---

## THREAD 1 — Does "hard readiness gates are an anti-pattern" transfer to autonomous agents? No.

**The human-side critique (the claim being stress-tested) is entirely practitioner opinion, and its safety mechanism is human adaptation.**

- **Mike Cohn, "The Definition of Ready and Its Dangers"** (Mountain Goat Software, 2016/upd. 2023) — https://www.mountaingoatsoftware.com/blog/the-dangers-of-a-definition-of-ready. Cohn's argument: a DoR that demands "100 percent finished before a story can be brought into an iteration" becomes "a huge step towards a sequential, stage-gate approach." His prescribed remedy is to let the team take rough/partial input and **"resolve remaining open issues during the iteration."** That remedy *is* the human asking-questions-and-adapting behavior — the exact capability the asymmetry says agents lack.
- **Scrum Alliance, "Pros and Cons of a Definition of Ready"** — https://resources.scrumalliance.org/Article/pros-cons-definition-ready. A rigid DoR "acts like a gate, blocking certain backlog items… until all of the pre-work… is completed," producing waterfall-like delay. Again: about humans, opinion-grade.
- **Contrary signal even within human agile — Jeff Sutherland** ("Ready: The Dynamic Model of Scrum," http://jeffsutherland.com/scrum/2009/07/ready-dynamic-model-of-scrum.html): the Scrum co-creator argues a *strong* Ready state prevents team thrashing and (per the Scrum Papers) roughly doubles velocity. So even for humans the anti-pattern claim is contested, not settled.

**The agent-side evidence shows agents default to confidently fabricating missing detail rather than asking — the asymmetry is real and empirical.**

- **"Learning to Ask: When LLM Agents Meet Unclear Instruction,"** Wang et al., EMNLP 2025 — https://arxiv.org/abs/2409.00557. Key finding: "due to the next-token prediction training objective, LLMs tend to **arbitrarily generate the missed argument, which may lead to hallucinations and risks**." This is the mechanism: the agent invents the under-specified piece instead of pausing.
- **QuestBench,** Kim et al. (MIT/DeepMind), 2025 — https://arxiv.org/abs/2503.22674. Models that solve fully-specified problems are weak at knowing *what to ask*: only **40–50% accuracy on Logic-Q and Planning-Q**, and the paper states "the ability to solve well-specified reasoning problems may not be sufficient… models have difficulty identifying the right question to ask, even when they can solve the fully specified version." Agents are weakest precisely at the clarification a human does reflexively.
- **"What Prompts Don't Say: Understanding and Managing Underspecification,"** Yang et al. (CMU), 2025 — https://arxiv.org/abs/2505.13360. Relying on the agent to infer the unsaid is unstable: under-specified prompts are "2x as likely to regress across model or prompt changes," with accuracy drops "exceeding 20%." So "the agent will just adapt like a human" is empirically fragile.
- **"Ask or Assume?"** Edwards & Schuster, 2026 — https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.26233. States the asymmetry outright: "human developers naturally resolve underspecification by asking clarifying questions, while current agents are **largely optimized for autonomous execution**." An uncertainty-aware multi-agent scaffold recovers most of the lost ground (69.4% resolve rate vs. a single-agent baseline on underspecified SWE-bench), i.e., the gap is real *and* addressable by adding a gating/clarification stage.
- **Practitioner convergence (opinion, but aligned with the empirics):** the spec-driven-development movement (Augment Code, Addy Osmani/O'Reilly, Microsoft Spec Kit) and CodeRabbit's "hidden cost of AI coding agents" (https://www.coderabbit.ai/blog/the-hidden-cost-of-ai-coding-agents-isnt-from-ai-at-all) argue agents need *more* upfront constraint than humans because "they don't stop to check whether requirements were vague… they just proceed confidently," and "catching the flaw before execution is consistently cheaper than unwinding a confidently wrong implementation."

**Design implication:** The anti-pattern argument is human-specific and rests on a capability (in-flight adaptation / asking) that agents demonstrably lack and are *trained against*. A readiness/plan gate for agents is not the stage-gate anti-pattern Cohn warns about — it substitutes for a missing human reflex. **Verdict on Thread 1: does not transfer; a plan gate is justified for agents even though discouraged for humans.** (Caveat: human-side material is all practitioner opinion; the agent-side empirical work is recent and partly 2025–2026 preprints.)

---

## THREAD 2 — Error compounding: why a *plan-time* gate is the highest-leverage place to catch a defect

**The geometric floor (independent steps):** end-to-end success = p^N (Lusser's Law / series reliability). The canonical worked numbers: at **95% per step, 10 steps → ~60%, 20 steps → ~36%**; at 90%/step, 5 steps ≈ 59% ("a coin flip"). These appear consistently across practitioner explainers (e.g., MindStudio, https://www.mindstudio.ai/blog/multi-agent-reliability-compounding-problem-77-percent) but the formula itself is standard reliability theory.

**The peer-reviewed law (strongest source):** "The Illusion of Diminishing Returns: Measuring Long Horizon Execution in LLMs," Sinha et al., ICLR 2026 — https://arxiv.org/abs/2509.09677. Verified verbatim: "even marginal gains in single-step accuracy can compound into **exponential improvements in the length of tasks** a model can successfully complete." Formally H_s(p) = ln(s)/ln(p). Critically, it shows real decay is *worse* than the independent model: "the per-step accuracy of models degrades as the number of steps increases. This is not just due to long-context limitations — curiously, we observe a **self-conditioning effect — models become more likely to make mistakes when the context contains their errors from prior turns.**" An early error doesn't just cost one step; it raises the probability of *future* errors.

**The empirical curve and decay model:**
- **METR, "Measuring AI Ability to Complete Long Tasks,"** Kwa et al., 2025 — https://arxiv.org/abs/2503.14499 / https://metr.org/blog/2025-03-19-measuring-ai-ability-to-complete-long-tasks/. Defines a model's "50% time horizon" (task length at which success = 50%); near-100% success on <4-minute tasks collapses to <10% on tasks >~4 hours. Horizon has been doubling ~every 7 months.
- **"Is there a half-life for the success rates of AI agents?"** Toby Ord, 2025 — https://arxiv.org/abs/2505.05115. Models a constant per-step hazard rate → success = (1/2)^(t/half-life), fitting the METR data. The mechanism: longer tasks contain "increasingly large sets of subtasks where failing any one fails the task."

**Cascades and the plan-time vs. execution-time trade-off:**
- **"Why Do Multi-Agent LLM Systems Fail?" (MAST),** Cemri et al. (UC Berkeley), 2025 — https://arxiv.org/abs/2503.13657. 14 failure modes in 3 categories (Specification, Inter-Agent Misalignment, Task Verification) from 150+ traces, inter-annotator κ=0.88. Notes failures "may have a cascading effect." **Caveat to flag:** the widely-quoted "42%/37%/21%" category split circulating in secondary blogs is **not** a clean verified primary figure — cite MAST for the 14-modes/3-category taxonomy and κ=0.88, not that split.
- **"When Should Users Check?"** Zhou et al., 2026 — https://arxiv.org/abs/2510.05307. A decision-theoretic model of confirmation frequency that formalizes catching errors earlier to reduce downstream propagation cost — the literal plan-time-vs-runtime verification trade-off.
- Mechanistic backing that one early error contaminates the whole chain: stepwise reasoning error-propagation work (e.g., https://arxiv.org/html/2412.11934v3).

**Design implication:** A plan is the root of the dependency tree; a flawed plan doesn't fail one step, it sets a low p that the whole N-step trajectory is multiplied against — and self-conditioning makes the realized decay worse than p^N. Catching a defect at plan time (one cheap critic call) prevents N steps of compounding *and* the error-amplification that follows the first mistake. This is the strongest economic argument for the gate.

---

## THREAD 3 — Separate critic vs. self-critique: the central question. Separate wins.

**(a) Self-preference / self-enhancement bias is real and measured — a model judging its own output is biased.**
- **"Judging LLM-as-a-Judge,"** Zheng et al., NeurIPS 2023 — https://arxiv.org/abs/2306.05685. Verified verbatim from the body: "**GPT-4 favors itself with a 10% higher win rate; Claude-v1 favors itself with a 25% higher win rate**," and "**GPT-3.5 does not favor itself.**" **Important honesty caveat (also verified verbatim):** "Due to limited data and small differences, our study cannot determine whether the models exhibit a self-enhancement bias." So this is suggestive, not conclusive — do not overstate it.
- The effect is corroborated by stronger later work: **"LLM Evaluators Recognize and Favor Their Own Generations,"** Panickssery et al., NeurIPS 2024 (oral) — https://arxiv.org/abs/2404.13076 — finds a "linear correlation between self-recognition capability and the strength of self-preference bias"; and **"Self-Preference Bias in LLM-as-a-Judge,"** Wataoka et al., 2024 — https://arxiv.org/abs/2410.21819 — traces the bias to low perplexity / familiarity (models prefer text in their own style).

**(b) Intrinsic self-correction (no external feedback) is flat-to-negative.**
- **"LLMs Cannot Self-Correct Reasoning Yet,"** Huang et al. (DeepMind), ICLR 2024 — https://arxiv.org/abs/2310.01798. Verified verbatim: "LLMs struggle to self-correct their responses without external feedback, and at times, **their performance even degrades after self-correction.**" Their definition of intrinsic self-correction is correction "based solely on its inherent capabilities, **without the crutch of external feedback**." The paper's central diagnosis is that prior "successful" self-correction smuggled in an **oracle label** (ground truth used to decide when to stop) — remove the oracle and the gains vanish or reverse. The model cannot reliably judge its own correctness.

**(c) Self-correction WITHOUT external grounding is the failure case; tools/grounding fix it.**
- **CRITIC,** Gou et al., ICLR 2024 — https://arxiv.org/abs/2305.11738. Bottom line: "our research **highlights the crucial importance of external feedback** in promoting the ongoing self-improvement of LLMs." Self-correction works when grounded in external tools (search for facts, code interpreter for debugging), not introspection.
- Survey corroboration — **"When Can LLMs Actually Correct Their Own Mistakes?"** Kamoi et al., TACL 2024 — https://arxiv.org/abs/2406.01297: "no prior work demonstrates successful self-correction with feedback from prompted LLMs" (except tasks exceptionally suited to it), while "self-correction works well in tasks that can use **reliable external feedback**." "The bottleneck is in the feedback generation."

**(d) Reflexion and Self-Refine — the discriminator: one secretly uses an external signal, the other is the cautionary tale.**
- **Self-Refine,** Madaan et al., NeurIPS 2023 — https://arxiv.org/abs/2303.17651 — is the *genuine* intrinsic method: "a single LLM as the generator, refiner, and feedback provider," no external signal by design. Its documented Achilles' heel is exactly self-bias, no reliable stopping signal, and degradation on hard reasoning without external feedback.
- **Reflexion,** Shinn et al., NeurIPS 2023 — https://arxiv.org/abs/2303.11366 — looks self-improving but its reflection is computed *on an external signal*: unit-test pass/fail (HumanEval, 91% pass@1), environment success (ALFWorld, +22%), reward. The abstract names "**external or internally simulated**" feedback sources explicitly. So Reflexion's flagship wins are not pure self-feedback — they ride on an external verifier/test. **Design takeaway: the methods that "work" are the ones with an external signal; the one that's purely self-referential (Self-Refine) is where self-bias bites.**

**(e) LLM-Modulo — the affirmative thesis: generators good, self-verifiers unreliable, use a sound external verifier.**
- **"LLMs Can't Plan, But Can Help Planning in LLM-Modulo Frameworks,"** Kambhampati et al., ICML 2024 — https://arxiv.org/abs/2402.01817. Core thesis: "auto-regressive LLMs **cannot, by themselves, do planning or self-verification**," but they generate good candidate solutions "albeit with no guarantees." The architecture pairs the LLM generator with **external sound model-based verifiers** in a Generate-Test-Critique back-prompting loop; "the soundness of the LLM-modulo framework is **inherited from the soundness of the critics**."
- The hard empirical backing — **"On the Self-Verification Limitations of LLMs,"** Stechly, Valmeekam, Kambhampati, 2024 — https://arxiv.org/abs/2402.08115. Verified verbatim: "**significant performance collapse with self-critique and significant performance gains with sound external verification**," across Game of 24, Graph Coloring, STRIPS planning (GPT-4); "merely re-prompting with a **sound verifier** maintains most of the benefits." This directly answers the central question: self-critique made it *worse*; an external verifier made it better.

**Design implication for Thread 3:** Do not let the executing agent grade its own plan. Self-preference bias (a), the inability to judge one's own correctness (b), and the self-critique performance *collapse* (e) all point the same way. The critic must be a **separate role**, and it is strongest when it can invoke **external grounding** (tests, schema/constraint checks, tool-verified facts, or a sound checker) rather than pure LLM introspection. A bare "second LLM, no tools" critic still helps by breaking self-preference and self-recognition, but the largest, most reliable gains come from giving the critic external verification capability.

---

## THREAD 4 — Plan verification before execution is an established distinct phase

- **Plan-and-Solve prompting,** Wang et al., ACL 2023 — https://arxiv.org/abs/2305.04091. Splits "devising a plan to divide the entire task into smaller subtasks" from "carrying out the subtasks," explicitly targeting **missing-step errors**; beats Zero-shot-CoT by a large margin. The foundational "plan first" result.
- **Plan-and-Execute agents (LangGraph)** — https://www.langchain.com/blog/planning-agents. A distinct planner + executor + **replanner**; claimed benefits include quality from "forcing the planner to explicitly 'think through' all the steps." Establishes the full plan as a reviewable artifact before any action.
- **ReWOO,** Xu et al., 2023 — https://arxiv.org/abs/2305.18323. Generates the *entire* plan before any tool call; **5× token efficiency and +4% accuracy on HotpotQA**. Evidence that committing to a complete, inspectable plan up front is both cheaper and (modestly) more accurate — the plan is exactly the right artifact to gate on.
- **"Understanding the planning of LLM agents: A survey,"** Huang et al., 2024 — https://arxiv.org/abs/2402.02716. Taxonomizes agent planning into Task Decomposition, Plan Selection, **External Module** (model-based verifiers external to the LLM), **Reflection**, and Memory — i.e., the field already treats external verification and reflection on plans as categories distinct from plan generation.
- **TravelPlanner,** Xie/Zhang et al., ICML 2024 — https://arxiv.org/abs/2402.01622. Verified verbatim: "**even GPT-4 only achieves a success rate of 0.6%**" (1,225 intents, ~4M records). Failures are dominated by inability to "keep track of multiple constraints." The brutal headline number for why naive agent planning fails.
- **LLM-Modulo on TravelPlanning,** Gundawar et al., 2024 — https://arxiv.org/abs/2405.20625. Verified verbatim: "Chain of Thought, ReAct, and Reflexion achieve a meager **0%, 0.6%, and 0%** with GPT3.5-Turbo," while the external-critic LLM-Modulo setup gives "**4.6x for GPT4-Turbo**" and lifts GPT-3.5-Turbo "**from 0% to 5%**." Note Reflexion (self-reflection) scoring **0%** here while the external-verifier architecture wins — a clean head-to-head for separate-critic over self-critique on planning.
- **The strongest plan-critic number — "Can LLMs Really Improve by Self-critiquing Their Own Plans?"** Valmeekam, Marquez, Kambhampati, 2023 — https://arxiv.org/abs/2310.08118. Abstract (verified): "**self-critiquing appears to diminish plan generation performance, especially when compared to systems with external, sound verifiers**," and the LLM verifiers "produce a notable number of false positives." The body numbers (Blocksworld): LLM plan-verifier ~61% binary accuracy, **38 false positives out of 45 invalid plans** (it approves invalid plans ~84% of the time), and end-to-end **LLM self-critique 55% vs. LLM + sound external verifier (VAL) 88%** — a 33-point gap. This is the single most direct piece of evidence for the gate: an LLM grading its own plan routinely waves invalid plans through.
- Plan-verification as a named pre-execution phase also appears explicitly in robotics — **VerifyLLM** (https://arxiv.org/abs/2507.05118) verifies a pre-generated action sequence *before* execution using temporal logic — and in **PlanBench** (https://arxiv.org/abs/2206.10498), which treats plan verification as a first-class task mechanically checkable via VAL.

**Design implication:** The plan is a concrete, inspectable artifact (you already store it in the ticket), and the literature treats verifying it as a distinct phase. The decisive caution: the verifier's **false-positive rate** is the metric that matters — a critic that approves bad plans is worse than none, because it manufactures false confidence. Where any part of the plan is mechanically checkable (file paths exist, referenced symbols resolve, acceptance criteria present, dependency/constraint satisfaction), route it to a *sound* check rather than to LLM judgment.

---

## Synthesized verdict

**Does the evidence support a SEPARATE-critic, plan-time gate for autonomous agents?** Yes, robustly, on four independent legs:

1. **It does not contradict the agile anti-pattern.** That critique is human-specific opinion whose safety depends on in-flight human adaptation — a capability agents empirically lack and are trained against (Wang 2409.00557, QuestBench 2503.22674, Yang 2505.13360). For agents, the gate replaces a missing reflex.
2. **The economics favor plan time.** Errors compound geometrically and worse (Sinha 2509.09677's self-conditioning; METR 2503.14499; Ord 2505.05115). A defect in the plan multiplies through the whole N-step trajectory; catching it with one critic call is the highest-leverage intervention.
3. **Separate beats self.** Self-preference bias (Zheng 2306.05685), inability to judge own correctness (Huang 2310.01798), self-critique *collapse* (Stechly 2402.08115), and LLM plan-verifiers approving 84% of invalid plans (Valmeekam 2310.08118) all say: the executor must not grade its own plan, and the critic is strongest with external grounding (CRITIC 2305.11738; LLM-Modulo 2402.01817).
4. **It's a recognized distinct phase** with demonstrated value (Plan-and-Solve, ReWOO's 5×/+4%, the survey's taxonomy, and the LLM-Modulo TravelPlanning sweep where the external-critic architecture beats self-reflection 0%→5%).

**Should it BLOCK or ADVISE?** A graduated stance the evidence supports:

- **BLOCK on sound, mechanical checks.** Where a plan defect is *deterministically* detectable (missing acceptance criteria, non-existent file paths, unresolved symbols, violated hard dependencies/constraints), the gate should be hard-blocking — these are "sound verifiers" in Kambhampati's sense, with near-zero false positives, and they are exactly the class of error agents cascade on.
- **ADVISE (soft / route-to-clarification) on LLM-judgment findings.** Where the critic is an LLM reasoning about plan quality, its **false-positive AND false-negative rates are non-trivial** (Valmeekam 61%; Zheng's own caveat that self-enhancement isn't even conclusively established). A hard block here re-imports the very stage-gate thrash the agile critique warns about — now driven by an *unreliable* gatekeeper. Better: surface findings as blocking-by-default-but-overridable, or convert low-confidence findings into clarifying questions back to the originating context (the agent analogue of the human "ask and adapt" that the whole asymmetry is built on).

In short: **separate critic, yes; plan-time, yes; block on what a sound verifier can prove, advise on what only an LLM can opine.** The single biggest implementation risk the literature flags is a critic that confidently approves bad plans (false positives) — so the gate's own verifier should be evaluated and tuned on its false-positive rate before it is trusted to block.

**Evidence caveats:** the human-agile side is all practitioner opinion; the Zheng self-enhancement figures carry the authors' explicit "cannot determine conclusively" caveat; the MAST "42/37/21" split is unverified secondary material (the 14-mode/3-category taxonomy and κ=0.88 are solid); and several supporting agent papers are 2025–2026 preprints not yet peer-reviewed. The core load-bearing claims (2310.01798, 2402.08115, 2310.08118, 2402.01622, 2405.20625, 2509.09677, 2306.05685) were each verified verbatim against the primary source.
